title: Who is Sam Farrington? What to know about UNH student behind college guns bill - Seacoastonline.com
content: On March 2, New Hampshire state Rep. Sam Farrington, R-Rochester, and about a dozen supporters walked into a Durham Town Council meeting fully armed. They were there to protest the council’s resolution against Farrington’s campus carry bill, which would prohibit public colleges and universities from regulating guns on campuses. The council said the bill would make the University of New Hampshire and the surrounding community less safe. Farrington disagrees. “You are tyrants because you are stripping lawful adults of their individual rights,” Farrington said to the council. Farrington's bill to allow campus carry has ignited a firestorm around the state. The bill has garnered over 800 online testimony submissions, with 643 against and 231 in support, and students have spoken both for and against the bill in Concord. A UNH Student Senate Survey found 85% of students oppose the bill, and in early March students and faculty held a walkout in protest. And on April 11, the NHGOP added campus carry to their platform. The man behind it? Farrington, a sixth-generation resident of Rochester who is a UNH senior and a first-term state representative. Farrington, who is in his last semester at UNH, said he wanted to run for office to keep New Hampshire a haven for freedom. He said he's long been passionate about the state and wants to keep it from becoming like its neighboring states of Massachusetts and Maine. “There's a reason why people are leaving Massachusetts to come to New Hampshire. It's because of our state and our motto, live free or die, it's because freedom is maximized in New Hampshire, and government is limited to its core purpose,” Farrington said. “I'm really passionate about freedom, about our state. I want to keep it on the trajectory that it has been. And I think we have something really special here in New Hampshire.” As a junior in college in 2024, he defeated an incumbent Democrat to take office. Farrington said his top priority as a state legislator is defending the U.S. Constitution, which includes the Second Amendment right to carry arms. This year, he has sponsored five bills related to firearms, including bills to allow them to be carried by people on snowmobiles, OHRVs and slow-moving boats and, of course, the campus carry bill. At the Town Council meeting in Durham, Farrington argued individuals have a “natural right” to bear arms. “Natural rights transcend government,” Farrington said. “Natural rights come from God alone, and so what God gives we cannot take away.” A dissenter called his statement “ridiculous.” Farrington says he asks those who disagree with him to respect his constitutional rights. “I respect your First Amendment right to disagree with me, and I would ask you to respect my Second Amendment rights,” Farrington said. Farrington said he is a big proponent of free speech. It’s part of the reason he held a Q&A about his campus carry bill at UNH on March 10, where he challenged students to "Prove Sam wrong” in an event reminiscent of the “Prove Me Wrong” debate series the late conservative activist Charlie Kirk would hold at universities. Farrington said Kirk is someone he “absolutely” looks up to as a role model. Farrington helped organize a vigil for the Turning Point USA founder following his assassination. “Charlie's approach to politics was having a conversation with people that he disagreed with, and I think we need to return to that,” Farrington said. “I hosted that event because I wanted to talk to the students that disagreed with me.” Other political heroes for Farrington include Josiah Bartlett, a former governor of New Hampshire and signer of the Declaration of Independence, and U.S. Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Kentucky, a conservative lawmaker who has become known for pushing back against President Donald Trump, particularly regarding the Jeffrey Epstein files. Along with guns, Farrington’s priorities include a constitutional ban on income tax, supporting school choice and broader issues like the cost of living and housing. On social media, he has posted about other things he’d like to do, including lowering the drinking age and “seizing the Portsmouth Naval Shipyard and reclaiming it as New Hampshire property.” Farrington said those issues are not top priorities, and he tries “to bring humor to politics." Yet, most of his social media posts are serious, he said. The Portsmouth Naval Shipyard for example, he said, is a bipartisan issue. In February, the New Hampshire House voted for a resolution whose sponsors include Rep. Joseph Barton, R-Grafton, and Rep. Jennifer Mandelbaum, D-Portsmouth, to claim the shipyard lies solely within the Granite State and that “the state of New Hampshire holds absolute right and title to those lands submerged under the navigable waters of the whole of the Piscataqua River in trust for the people of the state.” Farrington's current term ends on Dec. 2, 2026. He wants to stay in politics, but he said he doesn't yet know what he'll be doing in the future. For now, he said, he likes the New Hampshire House of Representatives. Like all members of the House, his seat will be up for election this November. There are no official candidates yet for his district; the filing period is from June 3-12. As for the campus carry bill, it passed the House in February by a vote of 188-165. A Senate Judiciary hearing for the bill is set for April 14.

title: Huskies Host New Hampshire For Tuesday Night Tilt - University of Connecticut Athletics
content: STORRS, Conn. – The UConn women's lacrosse team will look to secure its third consecutive victory, returning home to face the UNH Wildcats on Tuesday evening at 6:00 pm. The Huskies are coming off a dominant 15-4 road win over Xavier, controlling the game from the opening draw. UConn jumped out to a 5-0 lead after the first quarter behind early goals from Emma Dacey, Hadley Bethke, Ava Palazzolo, Rayea Davis, and Anna Magennis. Bethke led the offensive surge with a four-goal performance, while Palazzolo notched the first goal of her collegiate career and added another in the second quarter. UConn maintained steady pressure throughout, extending its lead to 11-3 by the end of the third. Megan O'Brien paced the fourth quarter with a hat trick, and Madison Sweeney added her first goal of the season to cap off the scoring. The Huskies' balanced attack and strong defensive effort secured their most decisive win of the season heading into tomorrow's matchup. New Hampshire enters Tuesday's matchup following a loss to Harvard in its most recent contest. In the defeat, the Wildcats were led by Charlize Collins, who recorded a team-high three points on one goal and two assists while also adding three ground balls and three caused turnovers in an all-around performance. Makayla Keys and Libby Boland paced the scoring with two goals apiece, with Keys also putting a team-high three shots on goal. The Wildcats will look to build off those individual efforts as they prepare for a tough road test in Storrs. UConn has historically controlled the series, holding a 14-7 record against UNH, including a dominant 17-5 victory in the teams' last meeting in 2024. The Huskies will aim to continue that success, while the Wildcats look to reverse the trend on Tuesday.

title: College Athlete Profile: Porter Hurteau talks racing at UNH and what fuels him on and off the course - St. Albans Messenger
content:
Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, a sophomore at the University of New Hampshire, studying chemical engineering, who’s also a three-season runner, began his running career at BFA-St. Albans, where he honed his skills, earning a spot with the Division 1 Wildcats. Why did you choose to study and run for UNH? For me, it was about finding somewhere that felt like home outside of Vermont. I grew up in a small town, and while I looked at the University of Vermont, I wanted an experience outside of the state. They have a great engineering program and a brand-new engineering building. I knew it was where I wanted to be. Why did you choose to study chemical engineering? Originally, I was going to go for mechanical engineering. I like to know the small details, and with chemical engineering, you’re working with the cellular level. It’s still a broad degree, and I’m getting a lot of opportunities and meeting a lot of new people in the industry. I really just wanted to try something new. Are you a car enthusiast? Yes, I like cars. In my junior year, I was sick of AP classes and decided to take automotive at Northwest Career and Technical Center, and it was super fun. I love working with vehicles—even every day cars. They’re machines, and you can figure them out with math. I love changing my own oil, headlights, and spark plugs. I love getting my hands dirty–compared to sitting in AP classes and listening to a lecture. My senior year, I took a class called Special Topics in Electric Aviation because it was taught by BETA Technologies. They were actual people from BETA who worked with Vermont State University. They took us through the simulators and through the class. I knew planes were cool, as were cars, and that’s how I got into chemical engineering. What is your goal for the use of your degree? I’d like to work with a performance division of an automotive company. You always have performance packages you can get, and I’d like to work on fuel systems and on making cars faster and more efficient. What do you enjoy about working with cars? I like math; I’ve been told my whole life I’m a good writer, but math has always been challenging for me, and I have to put time into learning it. I have a great memory, but I have to work at math and problem-solving. I like to sit down with math, work my way through it, and get the right answer. You know if you did it right, you did it right. If you didn’t, try again. There’s a sense of accomplishment for me when I’m sitting at a whiteboard and get the correct answer. What have you enjoyed about running for UNH? Honestly, I really enjoy being an athlete. So many people go to college to be students, and that’s why I’m here, but having something on campus that lets me escape the education side is awesome. I came to school and had a group of friends through sports. We spend at least two hours a day together, and we eat and spend time together. I love running and have done it competitively for seven years. It clears my head at the end of the day to go out and work hard with friends. We’re pushing our minds and bodies to the limit, and it’s awesome. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. What are some memorable moments in cross country after two seasons? There have been a lot. My first race was at my home course at Hard’ack in St. Albans, VT, and that was pretty special. I got to go back to the course I’ve raced and trained on forever. In my first race, I had a breakthrough. I ran a great race—was fourth for my team and top 15. We were there a month later for another race. The day before the meet, I got off the bus and saw my high school teammate Calvin Storm’s mom walking the course. It was special to be back, and the people in the town are like family. Do you have memorable moments from your indoor track seasons? Last season, I redshirted for indoor track, so this was my first indoor season racing. On Saturday, Jan. 31, I was at Boston University. I finished the race and saw my former teammates, Jacob and Teddy Tremblay, cheering for me from the UVM section and telling me what I had just run. Lucas MacKenzie, another former teammate, was also there. What are you looking forward to for your outdoor track season? I’m looking forward to this outdoor season. The conference will be at the University of Massachusetts, Lowell, where my former teammate Ehtan Mashtare runs. I’m looking forward to racing with my old teammates again. Ethan Mashtare, Jacob, and Teddy Tremblay all run in that conference, and Teddy just got welcomed to the America East, since he’s racing at UVM for his freshman year. We won’t be on the same team, but it will feel like home because we’ve raced together for so long. I’m excited to prove myself and show others what all of the BFA-St. Albans grads can do. What does it mean to you to see so many BFA-St. Albans alumni are racing at a high level, and what does it say about the program? I feel a sense of pride. Coach Mike Mashtare built this legacy over the last 45 years he’s been coaching, and I think it’s a testament to his knowledge and willingness to work personally with his athletes. He created this environment for growth in a cross-country high school team. We trained as hard as we could and still had fun. It really shows he was dialed in his training, and he’s willing to work with you. The strength we’ve exhibited in the America East Conference demonstrates the coaches' knowledge at BFA. Even now, Coach Mashtare shows up at the conference meets and sees us all racing. He’ll comment on form, and we know we can trust him. He’s there on the sidelines, and he’ll always be there for us. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Do you ever think about the Vermont connection while racing at Elle (Purrier) St. Pierre’s college? There’s definitely this feeling that her presence is always somewhere in the back of my mind. She’s a Vermonter, an Olympian, and a record holder. We saw her for the Hall of Fame induction last year. She’s ever-present, but the team and the coaches are focused on working with the athletes who are here in the present. What does it mean to you to grow up in a running family? Since we were young, we viewed our parents as role models. They’ve always been outdoors. We were sugaring in the spring, building huts in the woods, or going for family runs together. My dad has coached us up through high school and is coaching my brothers, Toby and Sam. It’s been nice to have that support. The first time I ran more than five miles was with my mother. I got dropped. We laugh looking back on it now that I’m a collegiate athlete. She’s always been there making granola bars to fuel us. My younger brothers have been an intrinsic motivator for me. I’ve wanted to lead by example, so I’ve always worked hard to be the best. Now that I’ve graduated from high school, it’s all about them and their last year of high school. We share our success and push each other. I’m proud of how hard they’ve worked and how our family has created this environment for us to realize our potential—so we’ve been able to do what we enjoy at the highest level. Do you have any wise words for younger athletes? Find something you love and are willing to put the work into. It’s hard to work hard for something you don’t want. If you have goals and want to reach them, work hard toward them. Try different things to reach those goals. It won’t be easy, but you’ll be willing to work a lot harder to achieve it. title: UNH's Grit to Grow helps teens explore careers, build leadership skills - Seacoastonline.com
content: Teens are invited to explore career opportunities and build leadership skills at the Grit to Grow Summit in the Lakes Region this June. The two-day event, hosted by the University of New Hampshire Extension in partnership with the Governor’s Task Force on Bring Back the Trades, will take place June 22-23, according to a community announcement. The summit aims to bring together teens, educators, employers, and community leaders to support young people as they consider life after high school. It will feature hands-on workshops, real-world career exploration, and discussions about college, trades, careers, and personal growth. “The saying, ‘It’s not just what you know, but who you know,’ is very relevant for our teens looking to determine their next steps along their career and education pathways,” says Kate Korbas, the state 4-H leader and youth development specialist, according to the announcement. The event will begin at Lakes Region Community College in Laconia, where teens will participate in interactive workshops focused on self-discovery, leadership, financial literacy, entrepreneurship, and storytelling. Sessions will cover topics such as paying bills, telling personal stories, and entrepreneurship. The second day will take place at the Inn at Mill Falls and is open to both overnight and one-day participants. Parents and caregivers are encouraged to attend alongside teens. During the second day, teens will engage in small group career exploration sessions with professionals from various industries, including skilled trades, healthcare, manufacturing, engineering, agriculture, hospitality, and government. Space is limited, and early registration is encouraged. The deadline to register is May 1. Scholarships are available for those seeking financial assistance. For registration and additional information, visit extension.unh.edu. This story was created by Kimberly Green, KGreen@usatodayco.com, with the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (AI). title: Ski industry workers are worried about climate change, UNH research shows - New Hampshire Public Radio
content:
New research from the University of New Hampshire shows professionals in the state’s ski industry are particularly worried about climate change. In a survey given last summer, 85% of New Hampshire ski professionals said they were either “alarmed” or “concerned” about climate change. Those results are much higher than the national average: 54%. More than half of the survey respondents did not think their industry in New Hampshire was doing enough to address global warming. The researchers said their results may point to motivation among ski industry workers to implement more sustainable practices and push for policy that could help limit the future impacts of climate change. “My hope is that the skiing community in New Hampshire recognizes that many of us share similar concerns about climate change,” researcher Elizabeth Burakowski said in a press release. “And, that we can collectively take action to reduce fossil fueled greenhouse gas emissions, use and produce energy responsibly and efficiently, and strategize practices that protect our winters while sustaining our outdoor recreational economy.” Get daily top stories from NHPR's newsroom with The Rundown. 
                        Check out all of NHPR's newsletters here. New Hampshire’s ski industry generates roughly $278.8 million each ski season, and supports 10,000 seasonal and 6,000 annual jobs, according to the researchers. Ski hills have tried to adapt, investing in snowmaking systems and branching into warm-weather activities like ziplining and biking. This winter was cold and snowy. But over time the ski season is shrinking, as New England winters warm. A statewide climate assessment from 2021 projected New Hampshire could see up to 50% less snow by 2099. The state averages around 100 days of deep natural snowpack sufficient for skiing each year. But, the researchers said, by the end of the century that could shrink to 60 or even 30 days, depending on future fossil fuel emissions. NHPR is nonprofit and independent. title: UnitedHealth (UNH) Stock Soars 8% on Medicare Advantage Rate Increase – Can Momentum Last? - MEXC
content: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) experienced its strongest weekly performance in seven months, surging more than 8% following an unexpected Medicare Advantage payment increase from federal regulators. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services confirmed a 2.48% boost to Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates for 2027. This represented a substantial upgrade from preliminary guidance that had suggested rates would remain virtually unchanged. The news triggered an impressive single-day rally approaching double digits. Market participants had been positioning for disappointing news, making the elevated rate an unexpected positive catalyst. Medicare Advantage has served as a critical revenue driver for UNH throughout the past ten years. However, escalating healthcare expenses combined with constrained government reimbursements had begun compressing profitability, making this regulatory decision particularly significant. Bernstein analysts responded swiftly following the announcement. The research firm elevated its UNH price objective to $411 while reaffirming its Buy stance. According to Bernstein’s analysis, the CMS ruling transforms what appeared to be a potential 4% drag on 2027 profits into anticipated earnings expansion of approximately 1.4%. That represents a substantial turnaround. On the same trading day, HSBC analyst Sidharth Sahoo elevated UNH to a Hold recommendation. While stopping short of a bullish call, the upgrade signals a recalibration of downside risks. The enthusiasm isn’t universal. Michael Ha from Baird maintained his Underperform stance, positioning him as the sole bearish voice among 31 sell-side analysts tracking the healthcare giant. Ha contends the payment bump may provide only short-term relief. His analysis highlights that fundamental challenges facing value-based care frameworks remain unresolved. That perspective deserves consideration. The Medicare Advantage segment continues facing a complex operating landscape, despite the favorable rate adjustment. UNH recently forecasted declining 2026 revenues — potentially marking its first annual sales decline in more than thirty years. Membership levels are projected to decrease across commercial, Medicare, and Medicaid segments. Shares remain down roughly 7% year-to-date and trade over 50% beneath their 2024 peak. This week’s advance narrows those losses, but significant ground remains to be recovered. Nevertheless, the consensus view on Wall Street leans positive. Among 31 analysts following UNH, 22 maintain Buy recommendations. The consensus 12-month price target implies approximately 17% potential upside from current trading levels. Optum, UNH’s healthcare services division encompassing pharmacy benefit management and care delivery operations, continues offering earnings stability while the traditional insurance segment navigates obstacles. Market participants are now focused on Q1 2026 results. Healthcare cost trends and any revised Medicare Advantage profitability outlook will be critical focal points. title: UnitedHealth (UNH) Stock Jumps 8% — Is the Medicare Relief Rally the Real Deal? - MEXC
content: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) had its best week in seven months, climbing over 8% after the US government delivered a surprise Medicare Advantage payment boost. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a 2.48% increase in 2027 Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates. That was a sharp improvement from an earlier proposal that had rates staying nearly flat. The announcement sparked a near double-digit rally in a single trading session. Investors had been bracing for the worst, so the higher rate came as a genuine relief. Medicare Advantage has been a core growth engine for UNH for over a decade. But rising medical costs and tighter government funding had started squeezing margins, making this ruling a much-needed development. Bernstein moved quickly after the news. The firm raised its price target on UNH to $411 and kept its Buy rating in place. Analyst commentary from Bernstein suggested the CMS decision flips what could have been a roughly 4% hit to 2027 earnings into expected earnings growth of around 1.4%. That’s a meaningful swing. HSBC analyst Sidharth Sahoo upgraded UNH to Hold on the same day. It was a measured move — not a full endorsement, but a sign that the risk profile had shifted. Not everyone is buying the optimism. Baird’s Michael Ha held his Underperform rating, making him the only analyst with a sell-equivalent view among the 31 covering the stock. Ha’s argument is that the payment increase may only be a temporary offset. He flagged that structural pressures on value-based care models haven’t gone away. That’s a fair point worth keeping in mind. The Medicare Advantage business is still navigating a tricky environment, even with the better rate. UNH recently projected a decline in 2026 revenue — which could mark its first annual contraction in over three decades. Enrollment is expected to shrink across commercial, Medicare, and Medicaid plans. The stock is still down about 7% year to date and sits more than 50% below its 2024 peak. The rally this week puts a dent in those losses, but the gap remains wide. Still, Wall Street’s overall read is constructive. Of 31 analysts covering UNH, 22 have Buy ratings. The average 12-month price target points to roughly 17% upside from current levels. Optum, UNH’s health services arm covering pharmacy benefits and care delivery, continues to provide some earnings stability as the core insurance business faces headwinds. Investors are now watching Q1 2026 earnings closely. Medical cost trends and any updated guidance on Medicare Advantage profitability will be the key items on the agenda. title: UnitedHealth (UNH) Stock Jumps 8% — Is the Medicare Relief Rally the Real Deal? - CoinCentral
content:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH) had its best week in seven months, climbing over 8% after the US government delivered a surprise Medicare Advantage payment boost. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a 2.48% increase in 2027 Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates. That was a sharp improvement from an earlier proposal that had rates staying nearly flat. The announcement sparked a near double-digit rally in a single trading session. Investors had been bracing for the worst, so the higher rate came as a genuine relief. Medicare Advantage has been a core growth engine for UNH for over a decade. But rising medical costs and tighter government funding had started squeezing margins, making this ruling a much-needed development. Bernstein moved quickly after the news. The firm raised its price target on UNH to $411 and kept its Buy rating in place. Analyst commentary from Bernstein suggested the CMS decision flips what could have been a roughly 4% hit to 2027 earnings into expected earnings growth of around 1.4%. That’s a meaningful swing. HSBC analyst Sidharth Sahoo upgraded UNH to Hold on the same day. It was a measured move — not a full endorsement, but a sign that the risk profile had shifted. Not everyone is buying the optimism. Baird’s Michael Ha held his Underperform rating, making him the only analyst with a sell-equivalent view among the 31 covering the stock. Ha’s argument is that the payment increase may only be a temporary offset. He flagged that structural pressures on value-based care models haven’t gone away. That’s a fair point worth keeping in mind. The Medicare Advantage business is still navigating a tricky environment, even with the better rate. UNH recently projected a decline in 2026 revenue — which could mark its first annual contraction in over three decades. Enrollment is expected to shrink across commercial, Medicare, and Medicaid plans. The stock is still down about 7% year to date and sits more than 50% below its 2024 peak. The rally this week puts a dent in those losses, but the gap remains wide. Still, Wall Street’s overall read is constructive. Of 31 analysts covering UNH, 22 have Buy ratings. The average 12-month price target points to roughly 17% upside from current levels. Optum, UNH’s health services arm covering pharmacy benefits and care delivery, continues to provide some earnings stability as the core insurance business faces headwinds. Investors are now watching Q1 2026 earnings closely. Medical cost trends and any updated guidance on Medicare Advantage profitability will be the key items on the agenda. title: For a Growing Number of Companies, UNH Is Home - New Hampshire Public Radio
content: This is a paid post. This content was paid for and produced by our sponsor. NHPR’s news and editorial staff had no role in this post’s creation. Learn more about NHPR’s mission here. When Airtho, a New Hampshire-based controlled environments developer, outgrew their office, they moved to a new yet familiar location: UNH. For several years, Airtho had worked with UNH’s John Olson Advanced Manufacturing Center on product development and testing, and half of their employees were UNH alumni. In 2022, they co-located their growing business with the Olson Center. “Moving to UNH has given me a more accessible pool of talented people and that has helped me grow the business pretty significantly,” says Airtho president Brandon Bogart, adding that the company has doubled its revenue year-over-year since co-locating. “We’re excited to be a part of this collaborative environment and to be close to the latest research, engineering talent and emerging technologies that help inspire the next generation of innovators.” Airtho’s success is hardly an outlier. A total of ten companies currently co-locate at UNH in Durham, a 400% increase since 2022, and more are in the wings. In fiscal year 2025, they occupied more than 13,000 square feet of space in UNH’s Olson Center, Parsons Hall, Rudman Hall, and elsewhere. On campus, they access state-of-the-art facilities, innovate with faculty researchers, and nurture a vital workforce. “UNH’s resources and reputation are attracting business and industry innovators to campus and the local area, who in turn generate economic benefits for the entire state of New Hampshire,” says Jennifer Miksis-Olds, UNH interim vice president of research and innovation. “Partners are coming to us from across the state and around the globe to access our state-of-the-art facilities, research expertise, and students.” For most industry partners, UNH’s facilities resources are the initial draw. Johnny’s Selected Seeds has limited heated greenhouse space and lacks sophisticated climate-controlled greenhouses like UNH’s Macfarlane Research Greenhouses. “We have high tunnels, like farmers,” says Johnny’s vice president of research and development Kevin Cook, but not a space to grow and develop a new, disease-resistant variety of cut zinnias in the winter, when they’re less busy. What’s more, UNH professor emeritus Tom Davis and his Ph.D. student Mamta Kajal are doing the very specific breeding research that’s likely to yield the zinnias Johnny’s hopes to produce. “This expertise is basically in our backyard,” says Cook, who notes that zinnia seeds were one of their strongest-selling flower seeds last year. Laboratory facilities are what drew RexChem founder Mark Spyvee, a longtime New Hampshire resident, to UNH after his company outgrew its incubator space in Massachusetts. That state, he acknowledged, “is not the cheapest place in the world to rent anything,” so he looked north and found his way to Parsons, where RexChem shares lab space with professor of chemistry Erik Berda. “Very quickly we came to understand that [students] provide a fantastic opportunity for us,” says Spyvee, who has hired nine undergraduates in the two years RexChem has been at UNH. “Without them, we wouldn’t have been able to do what we’ve done, which has helped us advance several drug discovery programs.” RexChem has several compounds in clinical trials, thanks to the bench work of students. The collaboration benefits the students, who “keep our robots busy by finding chemicals, weighing out chemicals in various prescribed amounts into what we call reaction vessels,” Spyvee says. “Essentially they have been doing what a professional chemist would do.” Co-location has brought some unexpected benefits to industry. RexChem utilizes some of the extremely expensive shared equipment in the University Instrumentation Center, nearby in Parsons Hall — and the company’s UNH student-employees get trained to operate it. Cook jokes that other breeders at Johnny’s Selected Seeds are jealous of the flower breeders’ access to UNH’s Macfarlane Research Greenhouses; he sees possibilities in utilizing the greenhouses for breeding biennials like Swiss chard, onions, and carrots. This work extends a four-decade relationship between UNH and Johnny’s Selected Seeds, which licenses cucurbit varieties bred at UNH. For Airtho, which counts other research universities among its clients for clean rooms and other technology, its UNH location gives them more credibility than “an industrial park in some small town in New Hampshire,” says Bogart. His employees and clients alike enjoy what UNH has to offer, whether watching a Wildcats football game or grabbing lunch in downtown Durham. “We knew that we would be a part of the Olson Center, but actually we've enjoyed being a part of the UNH community,” he says. “It’s more of an added bonus than I expected it to be.” If your business is interested in co-locating at UNH, contact Justine Stadler. title: UnitedHealth Stock Edges Higher After Medicare Advantage Payment Update - Sahm
content:
UnitedHealth Stock Edges Higher After Medicare Advantage Payment Update UnitedHealth Group Inc (NYSE:UNH) shares are trading marginally higher Friday morning following a policy update that improves the Medicare Advantage payment backdrop for insurers, helping sentiment across the managed-care group. CMS this week released its calendar year 2027 Medicare Advantage capitation rates and Part C and Part D payment policies, projecting a 2.48% increase in payments to Medicare Advantage plans, or more than $13 billion. When risk score trends are included, the effective increase rises to about 4.98%. That marks a sharp change from January's advance notice, which had projected only a 0.09% net average payment increase and had weighed on sentiment across the sector. CMS said the projected increase reflects several factors that affect Medicare Advantage payments, including growth in underlying medical costs, 2026 Star Ratings that will determine 2027 quality bonus payments, and updated risk adjustment policies. "Medicare Advantage and Part D should deliver meaningful value for the people who depend on them," said CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz. "These updates are designed to keep coverage affordable while helping ensure beneficiaries get real value from their plans." The countdown is on: UnitedHealth is set to report earnings on April 21. Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $372.19. title: Odd World: UnitedHealth Replacing Kraft in the DJIA (UNH, KFT, DIA, BAC, JPM, TRV, AXP, WFC, AAPL, GOOG) - Yahoo
content: In the age of health care reform, today's news will sound ridiculous to just about any investor. The Dow Jones Industrial Average (NYSEMKT: DIA) is making a crazy change among its 30 components. Kraft Foods Inc. (NASDAQ: KFT) is splitting itself up, so it should be no surprise that the food giant was going to lose its place in the DJIA. The shock is that UnitedHealth Group, Inc. (NYSE: UNH) is going to replace Kraft in the index. Are we or are we not in the age of health care reform? It is easy to argue that UnitedHealth is really just a financial company. Still, the DJIA already counts Bank of America Corp. (NYSE: BAC) and J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. (NYSE: JPM) in the DJIA. It also already has The Travelers Companies Inc. (NYSE: TRV) and American Express Co. (NYSE: AXP) in the DJIA. Wells Fargo & Co. (NYSE: WFC) would have been a far better choice than UnitedHealth. With health care reform, the health care insurance sector is only going to further consolidate. There already have been many mergers, and UnitedHealth itself is the product of many health care insurance roll-ups over the past decade or so. Investors will claim that they would have preferred to see Apple Inc. (NASDAQ: AAPL) or Google Inc. (NASDAQ: GOOG) added to the DJIA. The problem is that the share price is too high for an index that is a price-weighted index. Today's news just sounds a bit odd in the age we are living in.
